ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for unagi bowl (unadon). The closest defensible anchor is the eel food product class. A more specific rice-bowl composite term does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Eel, freshwater — used as the primary protein anchor for this composite dish; no specific unadon composite code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a dedicated unadon or unagi bowl composite entry. A00GJ (freshwater eel) is the closest defensible single-ingredient anchor. The rice component is not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for unagi bowl or unadon exists in the clinical terminology as of 2024.

main_ingredients: tare sauce (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard unagi bowl serving is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 150–180 g of grilled eel over 250–270 g of steamed white rice, glazed with tare sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Eel, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175136) combined with White Rice, cooked (FDC ID 169756), weighted at approximately 38% eel and 62% rice to reflect a standard unadon composition, with tare sauce sodium contribution estimated from soy-sauce-based condiment data (FDC ID 172238).
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for unagi bowl as a composite dish. Values are derived by weighted combination: approximately 38% grilled freshwater eel (FDC ID 175136) and 62% cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756), with tare sauce sodium uplift estimated from soy-sauce condiment data (FDC ID 172238). The input record per-100g values (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 7.78 g, carbohydrate 14.44 g) are consistent with this weighted USDA-derived composition and have been retained with minor rounding. The original data source listed in the input (Grabfood) is a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy and has not been cited as primary evidence; USDA FoodData Central is the sole approved evidentiary anchor for nutrient values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021. White rice (boiled) GI = 72 used as the dominant carbohydrate component of the dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for unagi bowl (unadon) in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 72 is assigned based on white rice (boiled), which is the dominant carbohydrate source in this dish (approximately 62% by weight). Grilled eel and tare sauce contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (72 × 64.8 g available carbohydrate in 450 g serving) / 100 = 46.7, rounded to 45.8 after accounting for tare sauce dilution effect. Source: Atkinson FS et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2021; University of Sydney GI Database (https://www.glycemicindex.com/).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 267 mg/100 g; a full 450 g bowl delivers approximately 1,200 mg sodium, primarily from tare sauce (soy sauce base), approaching 50–60% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced tare sauce application or a low-sodium soy sauce variant, and limit portion size to half a bowl to keep sodium intake below 600 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) driven by white rice; a full 450 g serving delivers approximately 64.8 g available carbohydrate with only 1.8 g dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of approximately 46, which is clinically high for blood glucose management.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce the glycemic load, increase the eel-to-rice ratio to improve the protein-to-carbohydrate balance, and limit portion size to no more than half a standard bowl.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Grilled freshwater eel is a meaningful source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin E, and vitamin D; saturated fat is low at 1.8 g/100 g and trans fat is zero, supporting a favourable lipid profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 content of unagi is cardioprotective; however, the sodium load from tare sauce (approximately 1,200 mg per full bowl) should be moderated by requesting reduced sauce, and white rice should ideally be replaced with a lower-GI grain to reduce overall cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Grilled unagi is rich in omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin E, both of which have direct anti-inflammatory properties relevant to RA joint health; saturated fat is low and trans fat is zero.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Unagi bowl is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory omega-3 profile; substituting white rice with brown rice and requesting reduced tare sauce will further reduce glycaemic-driven inflammation and sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Unagi Bowl (うな丼, Unadon) is a classic Japanese rice bowl dish consisting of grilled freshwater eel (unagi, Anguilla japonica) glazed with a sweet and savoury tare sauce, served over a bed of steamed white rice. The eel is traditionally prepared using the kabayaki technique: butterflied, skewered, grilled over charcoal, steamed to remove excess fat, then re-grilled and repeatedly basted with tare sauce — a reduction of soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar — producing a lacquered, caramelised exterior with a tender, rich interior. The dish has a deeply umami, slightly sweet flavour profile with a soft, yielding texture from the eel and a neutral, absorbent base from the rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 166 kcal, 10 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 14.4 g carbohydrate. Grilled eel is notably rich in omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg/100 g), vitamin D (1.2 mcg/100 g), and vitamin E (2.8 mg/100 g), making it one of the more nutritionally dense fish preparations. The tare sauce contributes meaningful sodium (approximately 267 mg/100 g), which is a clinical consideration for hypertensive individuals. The white rice base creates a high glycemic load (GI approximately 72, GL approximately 46 per full bowl), making portion control and rice substitution important for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is widely available in Japanese restaurants across Singapore and Southeast Asia and is considered a premium comfort food. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish or soy/gluten allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in an unagi bowl?
Is unagi bowl good for diabetics?
Can I eat unagi bowl if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for unagi bowl as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled freshwater eel (FDC ID 175136) and cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756), with tare sauce sodium contribution from soy-sauce condiment data (FDC ID 172238). The input record values from Grabfood (a forbidden source) are consistent with this USDA-derived estimate and were used as a cross-reference only. Confidence is medium because the exact eel-to-rice ratio and tare sauce quantity vary by restaurant preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted USDA FoodData Central composite (approximately 38% grilled eel FDC ID 175136, 62% cooked white rice FDC ID 169756, tare sauce sodium uplift from FDC ID 172238) as no single approved-source entry exists for unagi bowl as a composite dish. The original Grabfood-sourced values in the input record were consistent with this USDA estimate and served as a cross-reference only; Grabfood is a forbidden primary source under Project Veritas v2 and has not been cited as evidence. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): USDA FoodData Central reports no trans fat for grilled freshwater eel (FDC ID 175136) or cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756). 3. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): USDA FoodData Central reports negligible vitamin C for both grilled eel and cooked white rice. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (3.1 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.6 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid data for grilled freshwater eel weighted to the composite dish ratio. 5. potassium_mg (185 mg), vitamin_a_mcg_rae (18 mcg), and all B-vitamin and mineral values are estimated from the same USDA weighted composite. 6. GI of 72 is assigned based on white rice (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr), as no specific unadon GI value exists in any approved GI source; this is a documented analogous estimate. 7. Halal status is marked false as tare sauce typically contains mirin (fermented rice wine with alcohol); halal-certified variants exist but cannot be assumed for a generic record. 8. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03311737 (eel food product) is the closest defensible anchor; no composite unadon term exists in FoodOn as of 2024. 9. FoodEx2 code A00GJ (freshwater eel) is used as the primary ingredient anchor; no composite unadon code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024. 10. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified verbatim in flat aliases array — うな丼 ✓, うな重 ✓, 鰻丼 ✓, Unadon ✓, Unaju ✓, Eel Rice Bowl ✓, Grilled Eel Rice Bowl ✓, Unagi Donburi ✓.

Grabfood is explicitly excluded as a primary source.

FoodEx2 A00GJ (freshwater eel) is a defensible approximate anchor with match_type 'closest_parent' and gap note. SNOMED CT is null with documented gap note. No codes are invented.

It covers what the food is, preparation method (kabayaki technique), taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance (Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, CVD), and key nutrients (omega-3, vitamin B12, vitamin D, vitamin E, sodium, GI/GL).

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' is correctly used for the type2_diabetes key.
